narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70 and LSAT score of 29.50, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 41%, a risk high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on how your academic record stacks up against patterns from thousands of previous students, focusing on key indicators of bar success. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure was your first-year school decile ranking of 1st, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your classmates that year—far lower than the average 6th decile—and this strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your LSAT score, which, though below average, still nudged the prediction slightly toward passing by showing some standardized test capability. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a moderate pull in the positive direction, suggesting a baseline academic foundation better than the weakest performers, even if it wasn't standout. Overall, your low first-year performance outweighed these other strengths, mirroring patterns where early underperformance often correlates with bar exam challenges for students in similar situations. Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your first-year decile is a clear outlier dragging the odds down, while your LSAT and GPA align more closely with those on the bubble. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a whole—many factors like your full-time commitment and stable background are positives—but the model prioritizes these bar predictors to protect program outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your early college record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with updated credentials that address this key weakness. You have the drive from your background; targeting these areas could flip the prediction in your favor. Keep pushing—law school doors reopen for determined applicants like you.
